items.title: Classification of conjunctivitis
items.contentHtml:
Classification of conjunctivitis

  • Infectious: bacterial (purulent discharge), viral (watery, preauricular adenopathy), chlamydial (inclusion) or gonococcus (hyperacute).
  • Allergic: intense pruritus, eosinophils in conjunctival scraping, associated with atopy.
  • Irritative: due to physical/chemical agents (smoke, chlorine, foreign bodies), without significant cellular infiltrate.
  • Key fact: the presence of follicles indicates viral or chlamydia; giant papillae due to allergies or contact lenses.

items.title: Acute bacterial conjunctivitis
items.contentHtml:
Acute bacterial conjunctivitis

  • Abundant purulent discharge (morning with glued eyelids) is the hallmark.
  • Common agents: Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae.
  • First line: Topical moxifloxacin 3-4 times/day or topical gentamicin. In children, avoid aminoglycosides due to corneal toxicity.
  • ENARM trap: do not use topical corticosteroids in uncomplicated bacterial conjunctivitis (risk of corneal perforation if herpes is present).

items.foco: Viral conjunctivitis: watery discharge and lymphadenopathy
items.title: viral conjunctivitis
items.contentHtml:
Viral conjunctivitis

  • Watery discharge and painful preauricular adenopathy are the cardinal findings.
  • More common: adenovirus (epidemic keratoconjunctivitis), highly contagious.
  • Conjunctival scraping shows lymphocytes; there is no response to antibiotics.
  • Treatment: hygiene measures, artificial tears; do not use corticosteroids in the acute phase because they prolong viral replication.
  • Trap: acute hemorrhagic conjunctivitis (enterovirus 70) causes subconjunctival hemorrhages.

items.title: Gonococcal conjunctivitis
items.contentHtml:
Gonococcal conjunctivitis

  • Hyperacute: onset <24 hours postpartum, abundant purulent secretion, intense eyelid edema and chemosis.
  • Agent: Neisseria gonorrhoeae; Gram stain with intracellular gram-negative diplococci.
  • Neonatal prophylaxis: silver nitrate 1% (Credé) or topical erythromycin (most used today).
  • Systemic treatment: ceftriaxone 25-50 mg/kg intramuscular/intravenous (single dose) plus serum washout saline.
  • Trap: Gonococcal conjunctivitis can perforate the cornea within hours; requires culture and urgent systemic antibiotic.

items.foco: Chlamydial conjunctivitis: inclusion in adults and neonates
items.title: Chlamydial conjunctivitis
items.contentHtml:
Chlamydia conjunctivitis

  • Adults: inclusion conjunctivitis (serotypes D-K of Chlamydia trachomatis), mucopurulent discharge, lower tarsal follicles.
  • Neonates: appears 5-14 days postpartum, watery discharge that becomes purulent; associated with untreated maternal infection.
  • Diagnosis: conjunctival scraping with Giemsa stain shows basophilic intracytoplasmic inclusions.
  • Treatment: azithromycin 1 g single oral dose in adults; in neonates, oral erythromycin 50 mg/kg/day for 14 days.
  • Trap: do not use only topical; requires systemic treatment to eradicate reservoirs.

items.foco: Allergic conjunctivitis: intense itching and eosinophils
items.title: allergic conjunctivitis
items.contentHtml:
Allergic conjunctivitis

  • Intense pruritus is the guiding symptom; bilateral, seasonal or perennial.
  • Conjunctival scraping: eosinophils (diagnostic criterion).
  • Forms: seasonal (pollens), perennial (mites), vernal (children, giant papillae on upper tarsus), atopic (associated with dermatitis).
  • First line: topical antihistamines (cetirizine, olopatadine) and mast cell stabilizers (cromoglycate).
  • Trap: in vernal conjunctivitis, use topical corticosteroids only in severe outbreaks due to the risk of glaucoma and cataract.

items.title: Keratoconjunctivitis sicca
items.contentHtml:
Keratoconjunctivitis sicca

  • Tear film deficiency (aqueous or mucin).
  • Sjögren's syndrome primary (anti-Ro/La autoantibodies) or secondary (rheumatoid arthritis, lupus).
  • Schirmer test <5 mm in 5 minutes is diagnosis; fluorescein staining shows inferior corneal punctata.
  • Treatment: artificial tears without preservatives, punctual occlusion, topical cyclosporine 0.05%.
  • Trap: not to be confused with infectious conjunctivitis; The secretion is filamentous mucous, not purulent.

items.title: Follicular conjunctivitis
items.contentHtml:
Follicular conjunctivitis

  • Lymphoid follicles in the lower tarsus: immunological response to viruses or drugs.
  • Viral causes: adenovirus, herpes simplex, enterovirus; also Chlamydia trachomatis.
  • Pharmacological causes: idoxuridine (topical antiviral), atropine, pilocarpine, gentamicin (chronic use).
  • Differential diagnosis: conjunctival scraping (lymphocytes vs. eosinophils).
  • Trap: follicular conjunctivitis due to drugs disappears when the agent is discontinued; does not require antibiotics.

items.title: Giant papillary conjunctivitis
items.contentHtml:
Giant papillary conjunctivitis

  • Papillae >1 mm on the upper tarsus, associated with intolerance to contact lenses (protein deposits), prostheses or sutures.
  • Physiopathology: type I and intravenous hypersensitivity to protein or material deposits.
  • Symptoms: pruritus, mucus, intolerance to the lens, body sensation strange.
  • Treatment: discontinue lenses, topical cromoglycate or olopatadine; switch to daily disposable lenses.
  • Trap: not to be confused with follicular conjunctivitis; the giant papillae are specific to this entity.

items.title: Herpes simplex conjunctivitis
items.contentHtml:
Herpes simplex conjunctivitis

  • Usually unilateral, with vesicles on the eyelid and follicular conjunctiva.
  • Pathognomonic finding: corneal dendrites (branched epithelial ulcer with fluorescein).
  • May recur due to reactivation of the latent virus in the trigeminal ganglion.
  • Treatment: acyclovir topical 5 times/day or valacyclovir oral 500 mg 3 times/day.
  • Trap: topical corticosteroids are contraindicated in herpetic epithelial keratitis (dissemination and perforation).

items.foco: Neonatal conjunctivitis: prophylaxis with silver nitrate
items.title: Neonatal conjunctivitis
items.contentHtml:
Neonatal conjunctivitis

  • It is defined as conjunctivitis in the first 4 weeks of life.
  • Universal prophylaxis at birth: silver nitrate 1% (Credé method) or topical erythromycin (0.5%) or tetracycline 1%.
  • Chemical cause: due to nitrate silver (irritative conjunctivitis at 24-48h, self-limited).
  • Infectious causes: gonococcus (<24h), chlamydia (5-14 days), common bacteria (3-5 days).
  • Trap: all neonatal conjunctivitis requires culture; If gonococcus is suspected, treat systemic treatment immediately.

items.foco: Differential diagnosis: anterior uveitis and keratitis
items.title: Differential diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis

  • Anterior uveitis: deep ocular pain, photophobia, ciliary injection (periqueratic), miotic pupil, no discharge; cells and flare in the anterior chamber.
  • Keratitis: intense pain, photophobia, epithelial defect (staining with fluorescein), may have watery or purulent secretion depending on the cause.
  • Key data: in conjunctivitis, visual acuity is not affected (except corneal extension); discharge is the main symptom.
  • Trap: the presence of intense pain and photophobia suggests keratitis or uveitis; not simple conjunctivitis.
